heroOverview: An upper respiratory infection (URI) is an acute, usually self-limited infection of the nose, throat, sinuses, larynx, or a combination of these structures, most often caused by one of more than 200 viruses — rhinoviruses alone account for 30-50% of cases. Adults average two to three URIs per year; children under five average six to eight. The illness produces familiar symptoms of nasal congestion, runny nose, sore throat, and cough that typically peak within two to three days and resolve without treatment within seven to ten days. Bacterial complications — sinusitis, otitis media, pneumonia, or tracheitis — occur in a small fraction of cases but are the reason most antibiotic prescriptions are written in outpatient medicine, making accurate diagnosis of viral versus bacterial infection a cornerstone of antimicrobial stewardship.

keyStats.prevalence: Adults average 2-3 URIs per year; children under 5 average 6-8 per year (CDC, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; highest incidence in children aged 0-4 and the elderly; peak season September through April in temperate climates
keyStats.avgAge: No age restriction; incidence highest in children aged 0-5
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 17-25 billion URI episodes occur worldwide annually, making it the most frequent infectious illness in humans

definition: An upper respiratory infection (ICD-10: J06.9, acute upper respiratory infection unspecified) is an infectious process involving one or more of the anatomical structures above the lower respiratory tract: the nasal passages, paranasal sinuses, nasopharynx, oropharynx, larynx, and trachea. The vast majority are viral; rhinoviruses (picornaviridae) are the most common single pathogen across all age groups, followed by coronaviruses (including SARS-CoV-2), adenoviruses, parainfluenza viruses, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), enteroviruses, and influenza viruses. Bacterial primary URIs are less common but include streptococcal pharyngitis (Group A Streptococcus), pertussis (Bordetella pertussis), and diphtheria (Corynebacterium diphtheriae). The infection spreads by inhaling respiratory droplets from infected individuals or by transferring virus from contaminated surfaces to the mucous membranes of the eyes, nose, or mouth. Incubation is usually one to three days. The anatomy of URIs reflects that viral particles attach to and replicate in the ciliated epithelium of the nasal passages, triggering a local innate immune response that produces the swelling, mucus overproduction, and inflammation responsible for symptoms. Most URIs in adults and school-age children fall under family medicine; young infants with respiratory distress and immunocompromised patients often require specialist or emergency input.

typesClassification.description: The most prevalent form, affecting the nose and nasopharynx, producing rhinorrhoea, nasal congestion, sneezing, and mild sore throat without significant systemic illness; caused predominantly by rhinoviruses.

Inflammation of the posterior pharynx and tonsils causing sore throat, odynophagia, and often cervical lymphadenopathy; most cases are viral, but 15-30% of adult cases and up to 37% of children's cases are caused by Group A Streptococcus and warrant antibiotic treatment to prevent rheumatic fever.

Inflammation of the paranasal sinuses, typically following a viral URI that obstructs sinus drainage; most cases resolve without antibiotics but bacterial superinfection occurs in about 0.5-2% of viral URIs.

Inflammation of the larynx causing hoarseness or voice loss; in children aged 6 months to 3 years, the same process affecting the subglottis produces croup, characterised by a barking cough and inspiratory stridor.

A potentially life-threatening bacterial infection — most often Haemophilus influenzae type b before widespread vaccination — causing rapid-onset severe sore throat, drooling, and stridor that is a medical emergency.
Bacterial infection of the trachea, usually Staphylococcus aureus, occurring mainly in children and producing a croup-like presentation with high fever that does not respond to nebulised epinephrine.

primarySymptoms: Nasal congestion and obstruction — blockage of one or both nasal passages from mucosal swelling and excess secretions, the most consistent feature of a cold.

Rhinorrhoea — runny nose with initially clear watery discharge that may thicken and turn yellow-green after two to three days as immune cells accumulate; colour change alone does not indicate bacterial infection.
Sore throat or pharyngeal discomfort — ranging from mild scratchiness to severe odynophagia, depending on whether the virus or bacteria are predominantly infecting the pharynx.
Sneezing — a reflex triggered by nasal mucosal irritation, prominent in the early stage of a cold and a major route of aerosol transmission.
Cough — usually dry and irritating early on, sometimes becoming productive as the infection extends to the lower airways or as post-nasal drip stimulates the cough reflex.
Hoarseness or voice change — reflects laryngeal involvement; loss of voice (aphonia) can occur with severe laryngitis.
Low-grade fever — temperatures of 37.5-38.5°C in adults are common; higher fever suggests influenza, streptococcal infection, or bacterial complication.
General malaise and fatigue — a non-specific systemic response to infection, often accompanying the first two to three days of illness.
Headache and mild facial pressure — from mucosal congestion in the nasal passages and sinuses; severe or one-sided facial pain suggests bacterial sinusitis.
Watery or red eyes (conjunctivitis) — particularly associated with adenovirus, which causes the classic combination of pharyngitis, conjunctivitis, and fever.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild nasal dryness or tickling sensation one to two days before obvious congestion and rhinorrhoea begin.
A faint scratchy feeling at the back of the throat before frank soreness develops.
Increased fatigue or a vague sense of not feeling well a day before other symptoms appear.
Slightly watery eyes or mild light sensitivity in the early hours of a viral URI.
emergencySigns: Rapidly worsening throat pain with drooling, muffled voice, and difficulty swallowing or opening the mouth — may signal epiglottitis or peritonsillar abscess requiring emergency evaluation.
Stridor (a high-pitched noise when breathing in) at rest in a child or adult, which indicates upper airway obstruction.
High fever above 39°C that persists beyond three days, particularly with worsening rather than improving symptoms.
Stiff neck with fever and headache — possible signs of meningitis as a rare complication.
Breathing difficulty, accessory muscle use, or oxygen saturation below 95% — suggests the infection has spread to the lower respiratory tract.
In infants under 3 months, any fever above 38°C alongside respiratory symptoms warrants same-day assessment.

causes.description: More than 160 serotypes of rhinovirus account for 30-50% of all URIs year-round. The virus binds to ICAM-1 receptors on nasal epithelium, replicates best at 33-35°C (the temperature of the nose), and triggers an intense local cytokine response responsible for most cold symptoms.

Seasonal coronaviruses (229E, OC43, NL63, HKU1) cause 10-15% of URIs, more commonly in winter. They infect the upper airway epithelium and produce a clinical picture almost indistinguishable from a rhinovirus cold.

Adenoviruses produce 5-10% of respiratory illness and are unusual in causing multiple syndromes: pharyngoconjunctival fever (fever, sore throat, and red eyes), pneumonia, and gastroenteritis, often in institutional outbreaks among children and military personnel.

Parainfluenza types 1-4 are the leading cause of croup in children aged 6 months to 3 years, accounting for about 75% of cases. They circulate in autumn (types 1 and 2) and throughout the year (type 3), spreading by direct contact with respiratory secretions.

Streptococcus pyogenes colonises the tonsillar crypts and pharyngeal mucosa and triggers an intense local inflammatory response. It causes roughly 15-30% of pharyngitis in adults and up to 37% in children, and unlike viral pharyngitis it requires antibiotics to prevent rheumatic fever and glomerulonephritis.

RSV is the dominant lower respiratory cause in infants but also produces classic cold symptoms in older children and adults, particularly in autumn and winter. Its surface glycoproteins allow fusion with host cell membranes, leading to formation of syncytia in the airway epithelium.

Rhinovirus and similar pathogens survive on hard surfaces for up to three hours; touching a contaminated surface and then touching the nose or eye transfers live virus. This environmental reservoir explains why URIs spread readily in households, daycare centres, and offices despite the lack of obvious close contact.

riskFactors.description: Children average six to eight URIs per year because their immune systems are still building adaptive immunity to the many circulating viral serotypes and because they spend time in close contact settings such as daycare where transmission is efficient.
Attending daycare, school, or office settings concentrates respiratory droplet exposure; schools and daycare centres are well documented amplifiers of seasonal URI transmission in communities.

Both active smoking and secondhand smoke impair mucociliary clearance, damage the airway epithelial barrier, and blunt local immune responses, increasing frequency, severity, and complication rates of URIs by roughly twofold.

Conditions or medications that reduce immune function — including HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy, high-dose corticosteroids, and biological therapies — prolong URI illness, impair viral clearance, and markedly raise the risk of bacterial complications and spread to the lower airways.

URI incidence peaks September through April in temperate zones. Low humidity favours aerosol transmission and virus survival; more time indoors increases contact exposure; and cold, dry air may impair mucociliary defences.

Anatomical narrowing (deviated septum, nasal polyps) and conditions like allergic rhinitis impair sinus drainage and mucosal clearance, making viral URIs more likely to progress to bacterial sinusitis or otitis media.

Controlled human rhinovirus challenge studies have shown that sleeping fewer than six hours per night and high perceived stress both quadruple the risk of developing a clinical cold after exposure, mediated through reduced natural killer cell activity.

Immune senescence reduces antibody responses to newly encountered viral serotypes and impairs T-cell-mediated viral clearance; elderly adults are also at higher risk of progressing from a URI to pneumonia or exacerbated chronic lung disease.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 0-5, who average six to eight URIs annually
School-age children aged 5-12, who average three to five per year
Adults aged 18-64, who average two to three URIs per year
Adults over 65, who experience less frequent but potentially more severe episodes
Individuals working in healthcare, childcare, or schools with high exposure density
People living in crowded conditions or with young children at home
diagnosisOverview: Upper respiratory infection is almost always a clinical diagnosis based on symptom pattern, duration, and examination findings. No diagnostic test is needed for the typical self-limited viral cold. The critical decision is whether to test for and treat Group A Streptococcus (GAS) pharyngitis, which requires antibiotics, versus the viral pharyngitis that does not. The modified Centor score assigns points for fever, absence of cough, tonsillar exudates, and tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy — scores of 0-1 make strep unlikely and require no test, while scores of 2-3 warrant a rapid antigen detection test (RADT) or throat culture, and scores of 4 support empirical antibiotic treatment or throat culture confirmation. Influenza rapid antigen tests or RT-PCR are useful in high-risk patients or during epidemic periods. Sinus imaging (CT) is not recommended for acute URI but may be used when bacterial sinusitis complications (orbital cellulitis, meningitis, brain abscess) are suspected. Lateral neck X-ray or CT can help assess the airway in croup or suspected epiglottitis, though the clinical picture usually guides urgent management before imaging is obtained. Blood tests (complete blood count, C-reactive protein) have limited sensitivity and specificity for distinguishing viral from bacterial URI but may help triage the unwell patient. The main diagnoses to consider alongside URI are influenza (which produces higher fever, myalgia, and abrupt onset), allergic rhinitis (perennial or seasonal, with clear discharge, itching, and absence of fever), and COVID-19, which should be tested in febrile patients because its management and isolation requirements differ.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid Antigen Detection Test (RADT) for Group A Streptococcus
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects streptococcal antigen in a throat swab within 5-10 minutes, guiding the decision to prescribe antibiotics for pharyngitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A healthcare provider uses a cotton swab to firmly scrape the back of the throat and tonsils; mild gagging is common. A negative RADT in low-risk patients requires no further testing; in children a positive should prompt antibiotic treatment.

diagnosticTests.test: Throat culture for Group A Streptococcus
diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard for confirming streptococcal pharyngitis; sensitivity 90-95%, higher than RADT. Used when RADT is negative but clinical suspicion remains, particularly in children.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The swab is sent to a microbiology laboratory; results return in 24-48 hours. You may be started on an antibiotic pending the result if clinical risk is intermediate-to-high.

diagnosticTests.test: Influenza rapid antigen test or PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies influenza A or B in nasopharyngeal secretions, allowing antiviral treatment to be started within 48 hours of symptom onset in eligible patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A nasopharyngeal swab or nasal wash is collected; rapid tests give results within 15 minutes but have moderate sensitivity; PCR takes a few hours and is more accurate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes COVID-19 from other URIs, important for isolation guidance and, in high-risk patients, for starting antiviral therapy with nirmatrelvir-ritonavir within five days of symptom onset.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Nasal or nasopharyngeal swab; home antigen tests give results in 15 minutes and are widely available; PCR is more sensitive and preferred when clinical management will change.

diagnosticTests.test: Monospot (heterophile antibody) test or EBV serology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects infectious mononucleosis caused by Epstein-Barr virus, which can mimic streptococcal pharyngitis with severe sore throat, exudative tonsils, and lymphadenopathy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample or, for monospot, a finger-prick test; monospot is less sensitive in the first week of illness, so EBV IgM/IgG serology is ordered if clinical suspicion remains after a negative monospot.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures blood oxygen saturation non-invasively to detect lower airway involvement or early respiratory decompensation, particularly in young children, the elderly, and immunocompromised patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small clip-on sensor placed on the fingertip or earlobe gives a continuous oxygen saturation reading in seconds; no discomfort involved.

treatmentOverview: The majority of viral URIs do not require any prescription treatment and resolve with supportive care over seven to ten days. Current guidelines from the Centers for Disease Control (CDC), NICE, and IDSA unanimously advise against antibiotics for uncomplicated viral URI, sinusitis, or viral pharyngitis because they do not shorten illness, cause adverse effects, and accelerate resistance. Symptomatic relief is the cornerstone of management. Analgesics and antipyretics (paracetamol/acetaminophen, ibuprofen) relieve fever, sore throat, and headache. Intranasal decongestants such as oxymetazoline provide short-term relief from congestion for adults but should not be used for more than three days to avoid rebound congestion. First-generation oral antihistamines (chlorphenamine) reduce rhinorrhoea and sneezing modestly in adults; second-generation non-sedating antihistamines are not effective for viral rhinorrhoea. Saline nasal irrigation relieves congestion and improves mucociliary clearance without systemic side effects. For children under six, most combination cold remedies are contraindicated, and paracetamol dosing is weight-based. Specific antiviral therapy is available for influenza (oseltamivir, zanamivir) and COVID-19 (nirmatrelvir-ritonavir, molnupiravir), both most effective when started within 48-96 hours of symptom onset, and are indicated for high-risk patients — the elderly, the immunosuppressed, those with cardiorespiratory disease, and pregnant women. Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis is treated with amoxicillin for ten days, or penicillin V as a narrower alternative; this prevents rheumatic fever and shortens illness by 12-24 hours. Bacterial sinusitis that has persisted beyond ten days without improvement, or worsened after initial improvement, is treated with amoxicillin-clavulanate for 5-7 days in adults. Croup is managed with a single oral or intramuscular dose of dexamethasone, with nebulised epinephrine reserved for moderate-to-severe presentations in hospital. Epiglottitis requires emergency airway management and intravenous cephalosporin antibiotics.

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) and ibuprofen
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesics and antipyretics that relieve sore throat, headache, and fever during a viral URI. Ibuprofen has modest additional anti-inflammatory benefit for pharyngitis pain. Both are safe at standard doses in adults and age-appropriate doses in children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant symptom relief in approximately 70-80% of patients; ibuprofen reduces throat pain by roughly 1.2 points on a 5-point scale versus placebo in streptococcal pharyngitis trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal decongestants (oxymetazoline, xylometazoline)
medicalTreatments.description: Alpha-adrenergic agonists that constrict nasal blood vessels, rapidly reducing congestion. Restricted to adults and children over 6 years, for no more than three consecutive days to prevent rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide short-term relief in about 60-70% of users; no effect on illness duration.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antihistamines (first-generation: chlorphenamine, brompheniramine)
medicalTreatments.description: Mild anticholinergic effect dries secretions and reduces sneezing and rhinorrhoea; modest benefit for cold symptoms in adults. Cause sedation and are avoided in elderly patients and those operating machinery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce rhinorrhoea by approximately 25-30% versus placebo in controlled trials; clinically modest but perceptible to patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Penicillin or amoxicillin (for confirmed Group A Strep pharyngitis)
medicalTreatments.description: The standard antibiotic treatment for culture- or RADT-confirmed streptococcal pharyngitis; given for ten days. Prevents acute rheumatic fever and glomerulonephritis and reduces infectivity within 24 hours. Azithromycin or clindamycin is used in penicillin allergy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces the risk of rheumatic fever by approximately 70% and shortens symptom duration by an average of 1-2 days in proven GAS infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) for influenza
medicalTreatments.description: A neuraminidase inhibitor that, when started within 48 hours of influenza symptom onset, reduces duration of illness and lowers complication and hospitalisation risk. Indicated for high-risk groups: over 65, under 2, pregnant, immunosuppressed, or with chronic cardiorespiratory disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces influenza illness duration by approximately 1 day and hospitalisation risk by roughly 30-40% in high-risk patients (Cochrane systematic review).

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone (oral or intramuscular) for croup
medicalTreatments.description: A single dose of dexamethasone 0.15-0.6 mg/kg is the cornerstone of croup management; it reduces airway mucosal oedema within two to four hours and significantly reduces return visits and hospitalisation compared with placebo or nebulised budesonide.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces croup symptom scores by approximately 50% at 6 hours and halves the rate of hospitalisation versus placebo in randomised trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Saline nasal irrigation
alternativeTreatments.description: Isotonic or hypertonic saline solution flushed through the nasal passages clears mucus, allergens, and pathogens, relieves congestion, and improves mucociliary clearance. Well-supported by systematic reviews for symptom relief in both adults and children; safe with no limit on duration.

alternativeTreatments.name: Zinc lozenges
alternativeTreatments.description: Zinc acetate lozenges started within 24 hours of symptom onset have been shown in several randomised trials to reduce URI duration by an average of 2-3 days, possibly by inhibiting rhinovirus replication. Benefit is lozenge- and dose-dependent; zinc nasal sprays are avoided because of reports of anosmia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Honey (for cough in children over 1 year)
alternativeTreatments.description: A teaspoon of honey before bed reduces cough frequency and severity better than placebo and at least as well as dextromethorphan in children over 12 months, according to randomised trials. It is safe and widely available; contraindicated in children under 1 year because of botulism risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Echinacea preparations
alternativeTreatments.description: Some standardised Echinacea purpurea extracts have modest evidence of reducing URI duration and severity by roughly one to one and a half days; evidence quality is moderate and results vary by preparation. Generally safe in adults for short-term use.

whySeeSpecialist: Most URIs resolve with self-care or a primary care visit. An ENT specialist (otolaryngologist) should be involved when symptoms persist beyond three to four weeks (possible chronic sinusitis, nasal polyps, or tumour), when recurrent episodes suggest an anatomical predisposition, or when complications such as orbital cellulitis or mastoiditis develop. A paediatric or infectious disease specialist is appropriate for children with frequent recurrent infections, suspected immunodeficiency, or failure to thrive. Suspected epiglottitis requires emergency evaluation regardless of specialty.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute URI, a family physician, general practitioner, or nurse practitioner provides optimal care. For recurrent or complicated URIs, seek an ENT surgeon with interest in rhinology who offers in-office nasal endoscopy and access to CT imaging. Paediatric allergists and immunologists evaluate children with frequent infections to exclude immunodeficiency. Ask whether the practice follows NICE or CDC antibiotic stewardship guidelines and can perform rapid strep tests in the office.
preventionStrategies: Hand washing with soap and water for at least 20 seconds is the single most effective preventive measure; alcohol-based hand sanitiser is equivalent when hands are not visibly soiled.
Avoid touching eyes, nose, and mouth with unwashed hands, which is the most common route of self-inoculation.
Annual influenza vaccination reduces influenza-related URI, hospitalisation, and mortality; recommended for everyone aged 6 months and older.
RSV vaccination (for adults over 60) and RSV monoclonal antibody prophylaxis (nirsevimab for infants) are now recommended to prevent RSV-related lower tract disease.
Keep children with URI symptoms home from daycare or school for the first few days to limit community spread.
Maintain adequate indoor humidity (40-60%) in winter, which reduces aerosol transmission and viral survival.
Address modifiable risks: quit smoking, optimise sleep, and manage psychological stress, all of which measurably reduce susceptibility to URIs in controlled trials.
lifestyleModifications: Rest during the acute phase; forcing physical activity through a systemic febrile illness slows recovery and risks secondary bacterial infection.
Stay well hydrated with water, warm broths, and dilute juices; hydration thins secretions and supports mucociliary transport.
Use a cool-mist humidifier in the bedroom to ease nasal congestion and prevent drying of inflamed mucosa.
Avoid smoking and secondhand smoke exposure, which doubles the risk of complications and prolongs illness.
Maintain regular moderate exercise between episodes; a robust physical activity habit reduces URI frequency by roughly 25-50% in observational studies.
Get 7-9 hours of sleep per night; consistent sleep deprivation quadruples clinical cold risk after viral exposure.
Eat a varied diet rich in vegetables and fruit; vitamin C deficiency prolongs illness, and adequate zinc supports immune function.

recommended: Warm broths and soups, which support hydration and may have mild anti-inflammatory properties
Honey (for adults and children over 1 year) in warm drinks to soothe the throat and suppress cough
Citrus fruits and vegetables rich in vitamin C to support mucosal immune function
Zinc-containing foods (meat, shellfish, legumes, seeds) to support immune cell activity
Plenty of water and non-caffeinated warm drinks to stay hydrated and thin secretions
avoid: Alcohol, which impairs immune function, disrupts sleep, and can interact with cold medications
Tobacco smoke in any form, which worsens mucosal inflammation
Excessive dairy if it appears to thicken secretions (individual response varies)
Very cold drinks or ice cream if they trigger throat spasms in some individuals during active pharyngitis

exerciseGuidelines: During an active URI, the commonly cited 'neck check' rule applies: if symptoms are confined above the neck (runny nose, sore throat, mild headache) and there is no fever, light activity such as walking is generally well tolerated. Rest entirely when fever is above 38°C, there is significant fatigue, chest tightness, or breathing difficulty. Return to full training gradually once symptoms have resolved for 24-48 hours. Vigorous exercise during a febrile URI can prolong illness and, rarely, trigger myocarditis in influenza or enteroviral infection.
dailyManagement: Take analgesics regularly (by the clock rather than as needed) during the first two to three days to maintain consistent symptom control.
Perform saline nasal irrigation once or twice daily to clear secretions and support mucociliary transport.
Limit nasal decongestant spray to no more than three days; switch to saline-only after that.
Monitor for warning signs of complication: worsening fever after day three, new facial pain, ear pain, or difficulty breathing.
Stay home from work or school while febrile to limit transmission to others.
Complete the full course of antibiotics if Group A Strep pharyngitis has been confirmed, even once throat symptoms ease.
prognosis: The overwhelming majority of viral URIs are self-limited and resolve completely within seven to ten days in healthy adults, with complications in fewer than 2% of episodes. Bacterial sinusitis complicates roughly 0.5-2% of viral URIs and responds well to a short antibiotic course. Otitis media complicates about 2% of colds in adults and up to 20% in young children under two. Streptococcal pharyngitis, when treated appropriately with penicillin within nine days of onset, carries essentially zero risk of acute rheumatic fever. The most common persistent symptom is a residual cough, which can last two to three weeks after other symptoms clear due to post-infectious bronchial hyperresponsiveness. In individuals with underlying asthma or COPD, a URI is the most common trigger for an acute exacerbation, which can require hospitalisation. Immunocompromised patients and premature infants are at significantly higher risk of viral pneumonia and sepsis as complications. With appropriate management, permanent sequelae from a URI are rare in otherwise healthy individuals.
recoveryTimeline: Symptoms typically peak at days two to three of a rhinovirus cold, with nasal congestion and rhinorrhoea most intense, and then gradually improve. Most healthy adults are substantially better by day five to seven and fully recovered by day ten. A residual cough or mild fatigue can persist for two to three weeks. Streptococcal pharyngitis improves within 24-48 hours of starting penicillin, with near-complete resolution by day three to five. Influenza follows a slightly longer trajectory: fever usually breaks at days three to five, but fatigue and malaise can persist one to two weeks, particularly in older adults. Bacterial sinusitis resolves symptomatically in most patients within five to seven days of starting appropriate antibiotics.
complications: Acute otitis media — middle ear infection developing 3-7 days into a URI, particularly in children under 5; presents with ear pain, fever, and hearing reduction; occurs in about 2% of adult URIs and up to 20% in toddlers.

Acute bacterial sinusitis — bacterial superinfection of a viral sinusitis; suggested by persistent symptoms beyond 10 days or biphasic worsening after initial improvement; requires antibiotic treatment.

Pneumonia — lower respiratory tract extension, especially in the elderly, infants, and immunocompromised patients; influenza and RSV carry the highest risk; approximately 1 in 50 influenza cases in adults over 65 leads to pneumonia.
Peritonsillar abscess — pus collection around the tonsil, causing severe unilateral sore throat, trismus, and uvular deviation; requires incision and drainage and IV antibiotics.
Acute rheumatic fever — rare but serious complication of untreated Group A Strep pharyngitis; causes carditis, arthritis, and chorea; virtually eliminated by timely antibiotic treatment.
Exacerbation of asthma or COPD — URI is the most common trigger for acute exacerbations requiring emergency treatment or hospitalisation in patients with pre-existing airways disease.

supportResources.name: CDC — Common Colds: Protect Yourself and Others
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on URI prevention, treatment, and when antibiotics are and are not needed.

supportResources.name: NHS — Common cold
supportResources.description: UK patient guidance on cold symptoms, self-care, and when to seek medical help.

supportResources.name: NICE — Respiratory tract infections: prescribing of antibiotics
supportResources.description: UK national guidance on antibiotic use in acute upper and lower respiratory tract infections.

sources.title: Eccles R. Understanding the symptoms of the common cold and influenza. The Lancet Infectious Diseases, 2005
Shulman ST et al. IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis, 2012
Chow AW et al. IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Bacterial Rhinosinusitis in Children and Adults, 2012
NICE NG120 — Respiratory tract infections (self-limiting): prescribing antibiotics, 2018
Jefferson T et al. Oseltamivir for influenza in adults and children (Cochrane Review), 2014
Singh M, Singh M. Heated, humidified air for the common cold (Cochrane Review), 2013
CDC — Get Smart: Know When Antibiotics Work — Upper Respiratory Infections

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic rhinitis (hay fever)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic rhinitis produces perennial or seasonal nasal symptoms with itching, clear discharge, and sneezing but no fever or sore throat; a skin-prick or specific IgE test confirms allergy, whereas URI symptoms are infectious and self-limited.

confusedWithConditions.name: Influenza
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Influenza begins abruptly with high fever, severe myalgia, headache, and prostration disproportionate to nasal symptoms; a common cold is more gradual in onset and dominated by nasal congestion without the systemic collapse typical of flu.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma produces episodic wheeze, chest tightness, and dyspnoea triggered by allergens or viral URIs; a URI causes rhinorrhoea and throat symptoms without the airflow obstruction and response to bronchodilator that define asthma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epiglottitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epiglottitis presents with rapid-onset severe sore throat, drooling, tripod positioning, and inspiratory stridor — a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate airway management — unlike the gradual onset and mild course of a viral URI.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: The most serious downward extension of a respiratory viral infection, occurring in about 1-2% of influenza cases in older or high-risk adults, and a leading complication driving URI-related hospitalisation.

relatedConditions.name: Acute bronchitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Bronchitis shares the same viral pathogens as URI and is frequently a continuation of the same illness when infection descends to the trachea and large airways, producing a persistent cough.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Viral URIs are the single most common trigger for asthma exacerbations in both children and adults, responsible for at least 40-80% of all acute asthma attacks.

relatedConditions.name: Epiglottitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Bacterial epiglottitis, though rare since Hib vaccination, presents in the context of a perceived URI and must be rapidly distinguished because it is a true airway emergency.

relatedConditions.name: Peritonsillar abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: A peritonsillar abscess typically develops as a complication of bacterial pharyngitis or tonsillitis, requiring surgical drainage and IV antibiotics.

faqs.question: How long does an upper respiratory infection last?
faqs.answer: Most viral URIs last 7 to 10 days in healthy adults. Symptoms peak at days 2-3, then gradually improve. A residual cough can persist for 2-3 weeks. See a doctor if symptoms worsen after day 3 or last beyond 10 days.

faqs.question: Do I need antibiotics for a URI?
faqs.answer: No, not for a viral URI. Over 90% of URIs are caused by viruses, and antibiotics do not work against viruses. Antibiotics are only needed if a bacterial cause is confirmed — such as Group A Strep pharyngitis, bacterial sinusitis lasting more than 10 days, or bacterial ear infection.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a cold and the flu?
faqs.answer: A cold comes on gradually with nasal congestion and a runny nose as the main features. The flu starts suddenly with high fever, severe muscle aches, headache, and profound fatigue that are out of proportion to nasal symptoms. Flu also carries a higher risk of serious complications like pneumonia.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for a URI?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if fever exceeds 39°C or lasts more than 3 days, symptoms worsen after initial improvement, you develop one-sided face or ear pain, breathing difficulty or stridor, severe throat pain with drooling, or if you are immunocompromised or pregnant with any respiratory symptoms.

faqs.question: Can I go to work or school with a URI?
faqs.answer: If you have fever or feel too unwell to function, stay home until fever-free for 24 hours without medication. If symptoms are mild and confined to a runny nose without fever, you may work or attend school but should practice strict hand hygiene and cover coughs and sneezes to avoid spreading infection.

faqs.question: Is a URI contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most viral URIs are highly contagious from 1-2 days before symptoms start until about 5 days after onset. The virus spreads by inhaling droplets or by touching contaminated surfaces then touching your eyes, nose, or mouth. Hand washing is the most effective way to prevent spread.

faqs.question: How do I tell if a sore throat is strep or just a cold?
faqs.answer: Strep throat typically produces sudden severe throat pain, fever above 38°C, white patches or pus on the tonsils, and tender jaw lymph nodes — but no cough. A cold more often causes mild sore throat alongside nasal congestion and cough. A rapid strep test in the clinic gives a definitive answer in minutes.

faqs.question: Can a URI turn into pneumonia?
faqs.answer: In most healthy adults, no. But in the elderly, infants, immunocompromised individuals, and those with asthma or COPD, a viral URI — especially influenza — can progress to pneumonia. Seek medical care if new chest pain, breathlessness, or oxygen saturation below 95% develops during a URI.

faqs.question: What is the fastest way to recover from a URI?
faqs.answer: Rest, stay well hydrated, and manage symptoms with paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and pain. Saline nasal irrigation relieves congestion. Zinc lozenges started within 24 hours may shorten duration by 2-3 days. No treatment eliminates a viral cold, but these steps reduce severity and support recovery.

faqs.question: Do cold and flu remedies actually work?
faqs.answer: Symptom-relieving medicines like paracetamol, ibuprofen, decongestants, and saline rinses provide genuine relief but do not shorten the illness. Zinc lozenges started early may reduce duration. Vitamin C does not prevent a cold in most people but may slightly reduce severity. Antibiotics are ineffective and should not be requested.

faqs.question: Why do children get more URIs than adults?
faqs.answer: Children get 6-8 URIs per year compared with 2-3 in adults because their immune systems have not yet encountered most circulating virus serotypes. Each infection confers partial immunity. Close contact in daycare and school also amplifies exposure to respiratory viruses significantly more than typical adult settings.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with a URI?
faqs.answer: Light exercise like walking is generally safe if your symptoms are above the neck — runny nose, mild sore throat — and you have no fever. Stop exercising if fever is present, you feel breathless, or chest tightness develops. Vigorous exercise during a febrile illness can slow recovery and, rarely, trigger heart muscle inflammation.

faqs.question: Why does yellow or green mucus appear during a cold?
faqs.answer: Discharge turns yellow or green after 2-4 days as immune cells accumulate in the mucus. This is a normal part of the viral immune response and does not indicate a bacterial infection requiring antibiotics. Antibiotic prescribing based on mucus colour alone is a common driver of unnecessary antibiotic use.

faqs.question: Can stress cause a URI?
faqs.answer: Stress does not directly cause infection but raises susceptibility. Controlled challenge studies have shown that people with high perceived psychological stress are 4 times more likely to develop clinical cold symptoms after experimental rhinovirus exposure. Stress impairs natural killer cell activity and the interferon response.

faqs.question: What is croup and how is it treated?
faqs.answer: Croup is a viral laryngotracheitis in children aged 6 months to 3 years that causes a characteristic barking cough, hoarseness, and inspiratory stridor from subglottic swelling. A single dose of oral dexamethasone is the treatment of choice; it reduces symptoms within 2-4 hours and halves hospitalisation rates.

faqs.question: Are there vaccines that prevent URIs?
faqs.answer: Annual influenza vaccination prevents a significant proportion of influenza-related URIs and their complications. RSV vaccines are now available for adults over 60, and RSV monoclonal antibody prophylaxis is recommended for infants. No vaccine covers rhinovirus because of the large number of serotypes circulating simultaneously.

faqs.question: Should I use a humidifier when I have a cold?
faqs.answer: A cool-mist humidifier in the bedroom can relieve nasal dryness and congestion and may improve sleep quality during a URI. Maintain indoor humidity at 40-60% for best effect. Clean the humidifier regularly to prevent mould and bacterial growth. Heated steam vaporisers are not recommended for children because of burn risk.

faqs.question: When is a URI an emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care for stridor at rest, severe breathing difficulty, high fever with stiff neck, drooling with inability to swallow, confusion, blue-tinged lips, or oxygen saturation below 92%. In infants under 3 months, any fever above 38°C with respiratory symptoms is urgent.

metaTitle: Upper Respiratory Infection: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: An upper respiratory infection causes nasal congestion, sore throat and cough from viral pathogens. Learn when antibiotics help and how to recover faster.
